title: Acts 11
subtitle: Peter Defends the Gentile Mission; Antioch Founded

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Peter and Cornelius

sections.header: Verses 1—18 — Peter Reports to Jerusalem: God Has Granted Repentance to the Gentiles

heb.paragraph: the Jerusalem church's summary of what God has done: repentance is not merely moral reform but the gateway to life itself — the eschatological life of the new age. That God grants this to Gentiles is the chapter's theological climax.

places.name: Jerusalem — Apostolic Community
places.text: The Jerusalem church is still the theological centre of gravity in Acts 11 — the community whose approval of the Gentile mission is theologically necessary. Peter's report transforms potential controversy into corporate praise (v.18). The Jerusalem community's acceptance is the foundation for what Antioch will become.

places.name: Joppa — Peter's Lodging
places.text: Peter retells the Joppa rooftop trance as the starting point of his defence. The specific detail of the six brothers who accompanied him (v.12) functions as legal corroboration — he is not making a solo claim but presenting witnessed testimony.

tl.name: Peter's Report to Jerusalem
tl.text: Peter defends the Cornelius mission to the Jerusalem church. His unanswerable argument — 'who was I to stand in God's way?' — produces corporate praise rather than condemnation. The Gentile mission receives informal Jerusalem approval.

refs.note: The Cornelius episode — Peter's retelling makes the sequence clear: Spirit first, then baptism, then table fellowship. The order is God's, not the church's.
"John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit" — Peter quotes Jesus's own promise (v.16) as the interpretive key to what happened at Cornelius's house.
Peter's Jerusalem Council speech will repeat this same argument, citing the Cornelius episode as the decisive evidence for the Gentile mission without circumcision.

notes.note: MacArthur on "who was I to think that I could stand in God's way?" — this is Peter's most important theological statement in Acts. The question is rhetorical but devastating: no human tradition, however ancient, can nullify a direct divine act. MacArthur uses this to argue that authentic tradition must yield to clear divine revelation.

MacArthur notes the significance of the Jerusalem church's response: not grudging tolerance but praise to God. "So then, even to Gentiles God has granted repentance that leads to life" — they accept not merely the fact but the theology. This is the informal apostolic imprimatur for the Gentile mission before the Jerusalem Council formalises it.

Calvin on Peter's defence: he does not appeal to his own authority as an apostle but to God's sovereign action. This is Calvin's principle throughout — human authority is always derivative and must yield to direct divine action. Peter could have asserted apostolic privilege; instead he argues from divine precedent.

Calvin on "even to Gentiles God has granted repentance" — the word "even" (kai) marks the surprise. The Jerusalem community had not expected this; the Gospel's reach has exceeded their theological categories. Calvin uses this as a warning against limiting the Gospel to what we expect God to do.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that "these six brothers" (v.12) are named as accompanying witnesses. Luke's consistent attention to witness numbers (two or more witnesses) reflects the Jewish and Roman legal standards for credible testimony. Peter is not making a solo claim.

The NET notes the passive construction "God has granted repentance" — the same divine-passive used throughout Acts for salvation (2:47; 13:48). Luke consistently presents salvation as God's initiative and gift, not merely human response.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "These six brothers also went with me" — Robertson notes that Peter brings six witnesses to corroborate his account. This is the legal two-or-three witnesses requirement (Deut 19:15) more than satisfied: six independent witnesses to the same events. Peter is presenting legally sufficient testimony.

"Who was I to think that I could stand in God's way?" — Robertson notes the aorist infinitive kwlysai (to hinder) with the genitive absolute: "God having given them the equal gift." The argument is from accomplished divine action to obligatory human acceptance.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener notes that the circumcision party's objection was about table fellowship, not salvation per se. In Jewish practice, eating with Gentiles risked ritual impurity. The social dimension of Peter's crossing was as significant as the theological one — he had eaten in a Gentile home, making himself ritually impure by rabbinic standards.

"God has granted repentance that leads to life" — Keener notes the theological precision: repentance is described as something God grants (edōken), not merely something humans decide. Even the Gentiles' turning to God is presented as divine gift rather than human achievement.

hist.context: Peter's defence is a masterclass in apostolic argument: he retells the entire Cornelius narrative from his own perspective, ending with the unanswerable question of v.17: 'If God gave them the same gift he gave us... who was I to think that I could stand in God's way?' The circumcision party's objection was not about whether Cornelius was saved but about table fellowship — 'you went into the house of uncircumcised men and ate with them' (v.3). The social boundary was the real issue. Peter's response dissolves it entirely: God himself crossed the boundary first.

sections.header: Verses 19—30 — The Church at Antioch; Barnabas and Saul; Famine Relief

heb.paragraph: the first use of the name 'Christian' in Scripture (v.26). Formed from Greek Christianos + Latin -ianus suffix (as in 'Herodians,' 'Caesarians') — meaning 'belonging to or partisans of Christ.' The name was likely coined by outsiders as a nickname, not by believers themselves. It stuck permanently.

a recurring OT phrase for divine power in action (Exod 9:3; Isa 59:1; Ezek 1:3). Luke uses it to attribute the Antioch conversions directly to God: unnamed, unofficial men from Cyprus and Cyrene achieved something remarkable not by their credentials but by the Lord's active presence.

places.name: Antioch on the Orontes
places.text: The third largest city of the Roman Empire after Rome and Alexandria — population c.500,000. Located in Syria, it became the headquarters for the Gentile mission and the base from which all three of Paul's missionary journeys were launched. The church 'first called Christians' here will become the dominant expression of the faith outside Judea.

places.name: Tarsus
places.text: Paul's home city in Cilicia (southeastern Turkey) — a major Hellenistic city, seat of philosophy and rhetoric, and a Roman citizen city. Barnabas retrieves Paul from here (v.25) — possibly 8–10 years after his Damascus conversion. The years in Tarsus were not wasted: they were Paul's hidden preparation for global mission.

tl.name: Antioch Church Founded
tl.text: Unnamed men from Cyprus and Cyrene spontaneously preach to Greeks in Antioch. 'The Lord's hand was with them' — a great number believe. Barnabas arrives, then retrieves Saul. The disciples are first called Christians here.

refs.note: The scattering after Stephen's death — the event that sends these unnamed missionaries as far as Antioch. What the persecutors intended as silence becomes the seed of the world mission.

The Antioch church launches the first missionary journey — Paul and Barnabas commissioned by the Spirit through the gathered community. The church "first called Christians" becomes the church that sends the Gospel to the ends of the earth.

Paul's later confrontation with Peter at Antioch over table fellowship shows the church's ongoing struggle with the Jewish-Gentile boundary — the issue Acts 10–11 addresses is never completely resolved at the community level.

notes.note: MacArthur on Barnabas's reaction: "he was glad and encouraged them all to remain true to the Lord with all their hearts." The mark of a good pastor is that he sees grace at work — not suspicion but gladness — and his first impulse is encouragement rather than correction. Barnabas's character is consistently one of seeing the best and calling it forth.

MacArthur notes that the name "Christian" was likely a term of mockery in its origin — "Christ-people" or "Christ-followers" used by Antiochene pagans who saw a distinctive community. The fact that it stuck and became a badge of honour is itself a testimony to the community's visible distinctiveness.

Calvin on the unnamed missionaries: God does not wait for official structures before acting. These men had no commission from Jerusalem, no apostolic authorization — only the Spirit's prompting and the Gospel's power. Calvin uses this to argue that the church's mission cannot be limited to ordained ministers: the Spirit works through every believer who speaks the word.

Calvin on the famine relief: this is the practical expression of the covenant community across geographic and ethnic boundaries. The Antioch church — predominantly Gentile — sends relief to the Jerusalem church — predominantly Jewish. The relief offering is the economic embodiment of the theological claim that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET Bible prefers Hellēnas (Greeks/Gentiles) over Hellēnistas (Hellenistic Jews) based on manuscript evidence and contextual logic: if they were preaching to Hellenistic Jews (as in v.19), no contrast would exist. The contrast with v.19 ("only among Jews") requires Hellēnas — full Gentiles.

The NET notes that chrēmatisai (to be called) in classical Greek was used for official designations — names given by public authorities or communities. The "first at Antioch" suggests this was a new coinage, not the continuation of an existing label. The cosmopolitan population of Antioch had sufficient cultural awareness of the movement to coin a distinctive name for it.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "Began to speak to Greeks also" — Robertson notes the textual variant: some manuscripts read Hellēnistas (Hellenistic Jews) rather than Hellēnas (Greeks/Gentiles). The NET and most modern translations prefer Hellēnas, which makes this the first deliberate preaching to full Gentiles rather than Greek-speaking Jews. The distinction matters enormously for the history of the mission.

"The disciples were called Christians first at Antioch" — Robertson notes the passive chrēmatisai (were called) may indicate the name was given by outsiders (the Antiochene public) rather than adopted by believers themselves. Its subsequent adoption shows how quickly an external label became a mark of identity.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener notes the significance of the unnamed missionaries from Cyprus and Cyrene. They are not apostles, not commissioned by Jerusalem — they are ordinary disciples who spontaneously extended the mission beyond the expected boundaries. The Gentile mission's origin is grass-roots, not hierarchical, confirming that the Spirit works through the whole body.

Keener provides historical context for Agabus's famine prediction. Several famines occurred during Claudius's reign (AD 41–54), including a severe one in Judea around AD 45–48 documented by Josephus (Antiquities 20.2.5; 20.5.2). Luke's parenthetical confirmation ("This happened during the reign of Claudius") is one of Acts' verifiable historical anchors.

hist.context: The Antioch church is one of the most important turning points in Acts: men from Cyprus and Cyrene — unnamed, unofficial, not from Jerusalem — spontaneously begin preaching to Greeks (v.20). This is the first deliberate Gentile mission not initiated by apostolic directive. 'The Lord's hand was with them' (v.21) is Luke's theological endorsement: God preceded the official church structure. Barnabas's retrieval of Saul from Tarsus (vv.25–26) reunites the two men who will carry the Gentile mission to the ends of the earth. The name 'Christians' first appears here — in the most cosmopolitan city of the Empire, not in Jerusalem.

ppl.name: Peter↗ People
ppl.role: Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: Peter (Simon) appears in John 1:40-42 when Andrew brings him to Jesus, who gives him the name Cephas (Peter). This is his first mention in John's Gospel.

ppl.name: Barnabas↗ People
ppl.role: Paul’s first missionary partner; 'Son of Encouragement'
ppl.text: Barnabas (Joseph of Cyprus) is introduced as a generous land-seller in Acts 4:36–37 and is the one who vouches for the newly converted Paul to the suspicious Jerusalem church (Acts 9:27). He leads the Antioch church (Acts 11:22–24) and takes Paul as his partner for the first missionary journey (Acts 13–14). Their sharp disagreement over Mark (Acts 15:36–40) ends their partnership but doubles the missionary coverage.

ppl.name: Cornelius↗ People
ppl.role: Roman centurion; first Gentile convert; pivot of Acts 10
ppl.text: Cornelius is a God-fearing Roman centurion at Caesarea — devout, generous, and prayerful but uncircumcised. His angel-directed encounter with Peter (Acts 10) is the theological turning point of Acts: the Spirit falls on Gentiles before baptism, demonstrating that God shows no partiality. Peter’s report to Jerusalem (Acts 11) establishes the principle that will be formalized at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV\ESV“I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision, something like a great sheet descending, being let down from heaven by its four corners, and it came down to me.NIV\ESVBut the voice answered a second time from heaven, ‘What God has made clean, do not call common.’

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (c.93 AD)
src.quote: Josephus provides extensive first-century background for the political, religious, and social conditions described in Acts — the Herodian family, Pharisees and Sadducees, Roman provincial governance.
src.note: Acts’ historical details consistently align with Josephus’s independent account of the same period, supporting the narrative’s historical credibility.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS, 1QM)
src.quote: The Qumran community texts illuminate the diversity of Second Temple Judaism from which Christianity emerged — their eschatological expectations, community rules, and temple criticism parallel aspects of the early church.
src.note: The Scrolls demonstrate that the early church’s practices (community meals, shared goods, eschatological expectation) were not unprecedented within Jewish renewal movements.

src.title: Greco-Roman Travel Literature and Epistolary Conventions
src.quote: Acts’ travel narrative (ch.13–28) conforms to the conventions of ancient Greek travel literature, and Paul’s speeches follow rhetorical models documented in Greco-Roman oratory.
src.note: The Hellenistic literary conventions confirm Acts’ composition for a sophisticated Greco-Roman audience and support its historical reliability as ancient historiography.

rec.who: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Acts (c.400 AD)
rec.text: "Chrysostom’s 55 homilies on Acts are the most thorough patristic commentary, emphasising the Spirit’s active role and the apostles’ moral example for the church."

"MacArthur on "who was I to think that I could stand in God's way?" — this is Peter's most important theological statement in Acts. The question is rhetorical but devastating: no human tradition, howev"

tx.title: Western text (Codex Bezae D) vs Alexandrian text
tx.content: Alexandrian(P45, P74, א, B) is the primary scholarly text.Western(Codex Bezae D) is 10–15% longer, with additions that appear to expand and harmonise.
tx.note: Acts has the most significant NT textual variation. The Western text of Acts is not simply corrupt but may preserve early oral expansions. Most scholars follow the shorter Alexandrian text as more original.

tx.title: P45 and early papyrus witnesses
tx.content: The Chester Beatty Papyrus (P45, c.250 AD) is the earliest substantial Acts manuscript, generally supporting the Alexandrian text with some unique readings.
tx.note: The papyrus evidence has largely confirmed the Alexandrian tradition as the best text of Acts, though the Western text’s substantial additions remain a subject of scholarly investigation.

debate.title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.name: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.name: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.

coaching.tip: 'Who was I to think that I could stand in God's way?' (v. 17). Peter defends Gentile inclusion in Jerusalem. The evidence: 'The Spirit came on them as he had come on us.' Experience validates theology.

'The disciples were called Christians first at Antioch' (v. 26). The name emerges in a diverse, mission-minded church. Barnabas finds Saul; together they teach. Antioch becomes the Gentile mission's launching pad.

prayer_prompt: As the church at Antioch becomes the first place where believers are called Christians, consider asking God whether your life is so identified with Christ that His name is the most obvious label people would give you.
